title: Indirect instruction injection through trusted content
summary: A system retrieves content for a legitimate task, but instruction-like text inside that content influences planning or tool use as though it came from the user or developer.
preconditions: The system processes content that is not fully controlled by the user or operator.
Retrieved content and trusted instructions are available to the same planning component.
The system can call at least one tool or produce an externally consumed decision.
No independent control reliably binds the proposed action to the original user intent.
attack_path: The user requests a legitimate task that requires reading a document, message, page, or tool result.
The retrieved material contains text that resembles a command, policy update, or required next step.
The planner treats that text as control input rather than untrusted data.
The planner proposes an action that serves the content author rather than the user's stated task.
A tool or downstream process executes the action using authority already available to the system.
observable_signals: A tool call cannot be traced to an explicit requirement in the user's request.
The action rationale repeats unusual language found only in retrieved content.
The proposed target, recipient, resource, or scope changes after untrusted content is read.
The system accesses information that was not needed to complete the original task.
The same content causes different behavior when tool access is enabled compared with read-only execution.
limitations: The presence of instruction-like text is not proof that the system followed it.
Model behavior varies by model version, context construction, tool design, and surrounding policy controls.
Content filtering may reduce common attacks but can also reject legitimate procedural documents.
A safe final outcome does not reveal whether the model resisted or a later control blocked the action.
open_questions: Which provenance formats remain effective after summarization and multi-step handoff?
How should systems measure whether an action is semantically tied to the original user intent?
What level of tool isolation preserves useful task completion while limiting attacker-controlled effects?
How well do deterministic policy gates hold against adaptive attacks in realistic workflows?
